schools.summary: Gill: Moses, with the elders of Israel, commanded the people.
Keil & Delitzsch: part of the conclusion and renewal of the covenant in the land of Moab.

themes: Keep all the commandments which I command you this day
a united charge to obedience
the covenant renewed, the people bound (Heb. 13:17)

schools.summary: Gill: 'on the day' means about that time, soon after they passed, though not executed until after Ai was destroyed; the great stones were to be plastered.

themes: thou shalt set thee up great stones, and plaister them with plaister
the law set up among the first acts in the land
God's word given the first place (Josh. 8:30)

schools.summary: Gill: not the whole book of Deuteronomy but the laws of God, perhaps a summary or the ten commandments; Josephus thought the blessings and curses; written when they passed into the land.

themes: thou shalt write upon them all the words of this law
the law made a public, abiding witness
the word set plainly, that he may run that readeth (Hab. 2:2)

schools.summary: Gill: some time after they had passed Jordan.
Keil & Delitzsch: the stones raised on Mount Ebal and plastered.

themes: ye shall set up these stones... in mount Ebal
the law set on the mount of cursing
the law condemns; blessing sought beyond it (Gal. 3:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the altar was on the same mountain, though not of the same stones; Joshua so built it; no iron tool was to be lifted upon the stones.

themes: thou shalt build an altar... of stones: thou shalt not lift up any iron tool upon them
the unhewn altar of grace beside the condemning law
approach not by man's work but appointed sacrifice (Exod. 20:25)

schools.summary: Gill: the altar was built of whole stones, as Joshua did.
Ellicott: the idea of the burnt offering is the dedication of man's life to God.
Wesley: the whole, unhewn stones, the law written on them and the altar joined.

themes: Thou shalt build the altar... of whole stones; and thou shalt offer burnt offerings thereon
the burnt offering man's entire dedication to God
Christ offered wholly, that we be accepted (Rom. 12:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the peace offering — part to God, part to the priest, and the rest to the owner, eaten with his friends.
Ellicott: these were offerings for health and peace.

themes: thou shalt offer peace offerings, and shalt eat there, and rejoice before the LORD
reconciliation and joyful communion the end of sacrifice
Christ our peace, the feast after atonement (Eph. 2:14)

schools.summary: Gill: written on the first stones set up before, on which the words were inscribed before the altar was erected, and to be written 'very plainly.'

themes: thou shalt write upon the stones all the words of this law very plainly
God's word made clear and accessible
the word not hidden, none excused (Deut. 30:11)

schools.summary: Gill: the priests who were Levites joined with Moses in the exhortation; this day they were become the people of the LORD.

themes: this day thou art become the people of the LORD thy God
the covenant renewed and owned anew
a fresh consecration to the Lord (2 Cor. 8:5)

schools.summary: Gill: obeying whatever he directs in his word, by his prophets, and especially by his Son, eminently called the Word of the Lord.
Keil & Delitzsch: the charge to obey and do his commandments and statutes.

themes: Thou shalt therefore obey the voice of the LORD thy God, and do his commandments
obedience flowing from belonging to him
hearing him supremely in his Son (Matt. 17:5)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses gave the orders to set up stones, write the law, build the altar, and offer sacrifices.
Henry: when the law was written to be seen and read by all, the people were to be solemnly called to it.

themes: And Moses charged the people the same day
the law pressed home, not merely displayed
the word brought home to the conscience (Neh. 8:8)

schools.summary: Gill: these tribes were to stand on Gerizim to bless.
Keil & Delitzsch: name the six tribes for blessing on Gerizim.

themes: These shall stand upon mount Gerizim to bless the people
the whole nation engaged in the covenant
the two ways set before them (Deut. 30:19)

schools.summary: Gill: Ebal was dry and rocky, barren, like the earth that bears thorns and is nigh unto cursing — a fit place to curse and emblem of those to be cursed.
Wesley: these tribes appointed for the curse.

themes: And these shall stand upon mount Ebal to curse
the barren mount an emblem of the cursed
the law's curse for the transgressor (Heb. 6:8)

schools.summary: Gill: not the whole tribe but the priests placed in the valley between the mounts, who pronounced both blessings and curses with a loud voice, in the hearing of all.

themes: the Levites shall speak, and say unto all the men of Israel with a loud voice
the covenant openly proclaimed
the priests ministering the word (Mal. 2:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the blessings and their form are not recorded, to put men upon seeking them elsewhere, only to be had in Christ; the curse falls on secret idolatry; all the people answered, Amen.

themes: Cursed be the man that maketh any graven or molten image... in a secret place. And all the people shall... say, Amen
God judging secret idolatry
the blessings sought only in Christ (Gal. 3:10)

schools.summary: Gill: of one who secretly despises them in his heart, mocking by looks and gestures unseen; open cursing was punishable by the magistrate with death.

themes: Cursed be he that setteth light by his father or his mother
the secret dishonouring of parents cursed
the heart searched, beyond man's reach (Prov. 30:17)

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: the removing of the landmark backward to steal ground, commonly done secretly.
Clarke: refers to the earlier law (Deut. 19:14).

themes: Cursed be he that removeth his neighbour's landmark
the secret fraud of stealing land cursed
unseen by men but open before God (Prov. 22:28)

schools.summary: Gill: the misdirecting of the blind knowingly, or laying a stumbling block; the Targum extends it to misleading a traveller who, like a blind man, knows not his way; Jarchi takes it of blinding any in a matter.

themes: Cursed be he that maketh the blind to wander out of the way
the cruel betrayal of trust cursed
no stumblingblock before the blind (Lev. 19:14)

schools.summary: Gill: the perverting of the judgment of the stranger, fatherless, and widow; all the people said, Amen.

themes: Cursed be he that perverteth the judgment of the stranger, fatherless, and widow
injustice to the helpless cursed
God a father of the fatherless (Ps. 68:5)

schools.summary: Gill: as Reuben did, forbidden in the law (Lev. 18:8); all the people said, Amen.

themes: Cursed be he that lieth with his father's wife
the secret defilement of the family cursed
no uncleanness escapes God's judgment (Heb. 13:4)

schools.summary: Gill: refers to the law forbidding it (Lev. 18:23); all the people said, Amen.

themes: Cursed be he that lieth with any manner of beast
the unnatural confusion cursed
accursed before God who sees the darkest deeds (Lev. 18:23)

schools.summary: Gill: forbidden in the law (Lev. 18:9); all the people said, Amen.

themes: Cursed be he that lieth with his sister
the secret breach of kinship cursed
the hidden violation judged by God (Ezek. 22:11)

schools.summary: Gill: this completes the list; all these incestuous or brutal copulations may well be supposed to be done in secret; all the people said, Amen.

themes: Cursed be he that lieth with his mother in law
the secret sins of the flesh cursed
God brings every secret thing into judgment (Eccl. 12:14)

schools.summary: Gill: the smiting and killing of a neighbour secretly, the murder unknown; some take it of smiting with the tongue, secret backbiting and slander, 'the third tongue' of the Targum.

themes: Cursed be he that smiteth his neighbour secretly
the hidden blow or secret slander cursed
privy slander cut off by God (Ps. 101:5)

schools.summary: Gill: the assassin who murders privately for hire, or the judge who takes a bribe to condemn the innocent, or, as he thinks, a false witness who swears away a man's life.

themes: Cursed be he that taketh reward to slay an innocent person
the trafficking in innocent blood cursed
the LORD the defence of the innocent (Ps. 94:21-22)

schools.summary: Gill: whoever does not perfectly and constantly perform all the law requires; the law requires perfect and constant obedience, and curses upon failure — the apostle so producing this passage (Gal. 3:10).

themes: Cursed be he that confirmeth not all the words of this law to do them
the universal curse on imperfect obedience
Christ made a curse for us (Gal. 3:10, 13)
